questions.prompt: In a relative frequency table, the relative frequencies of all classes must:
choices: equal the sample size
sum to 1 (allowing for rounding)
each be at least 0.05
increase from class to class

questions.prompt: A histogram of household incomes shows most bars clustered at lower values with a long tail stretching toward high incomes. The distribution is:
choices: left-skewed
symmetric
right-skewed
bimodal

questions.prompt: The bars of a histogram touch, but the bars of a bar chart do not. The reason is:
choices: histograms show continuous numeric intervals; bar charts show separate categories
histograms always have more classes than bar charts
bar charts are only used for small samples
it is purely a style convention with no meaning

questions.prompt: In a cumulative relative frequency table for study hours, the row for the class ending at 10 hours shows 0.62. This means:
choices: 62% of students study exactly 10 hours
62% of students study 10 hours or less
62% of students study more than 10 hours
the class from 8 to 10 hours contains 62% of students

questions.prompt: Scores on a very easy exam, where most students score high and a few score very low, produce a distribution that is:
choices: right-skewed, because most scores are high
left-skewed, because the tail stretches toward low scores
symmetric, because the exam had a maximum score
uniform, because everyone took the same exam

questions.prompt: A histogram appears to have two distinct peaks. The most likely explanation worth investigating is:
choices: the class width was too wide
the data contains two different groups mixed together
the data was collected without randomization
relative frequencies were used instead of frequencies

questions.prompt: A dataset of 40 quiz scores includes 6 scores in the class 70–79. Compute the relative frequency of that class, and explain what a cumulative relative frequency of 0.85 for that same class would tell you.
questions.modelAnswer: The relative frequency is 6/40 = 0.15, meaning 15% of scores fall in the 70–79 class. A cumulative relative frequency of 0.85 for that class means 85% of all scores are 79 or below — only 15% of students scored 80 or above.

rubric.description: Explains cumulative 0.85 as the fraction of scores at or below the top of that class

questions.prompt: Sketch in words what the histogram of house prices in a city typically looks like, name the shape, and predict how the mean will compare to the median.
questions.modelAnswer: Most houses cluster in a low-to-middle price range, with a small number of very expensive homes stretching a long tail to the right — a right-skewed distribution. Because the long right tail pulls the mean upward, the mean price will be higher than the median price.

rubric.description: Identifies the distribution as right-skewed (long tail toward high prices)
Predicts mean > median because the tail pulls the mean

questions.prompt: A colleague makes a histogram of 200 response times using 3 classes, sees no interesting pattern, and concludes the data is featureless. What would you check before accepting that conclusion, and why?
questions.modelAnswer: Check the class width: with only 3 classes, real features get smoothed away. Re-draw the histogram with more classes (roughly 5–15 for most datasets) and see whether skew, outliers, or multiple peaks appear. A conclusion about shape should hold across reasonable choices of class width, not just one.

rubric.description: Identifies too-few/too-wide classes as the problem and suggests re-binning (≈5–15 classes)
Notes that a real feature should survive different reasonable class choices

questions.prompt: You have two datasets: the favorite music genre of 300 students, and the daily minutes those students spend listening. For each dataset, name the appropriate graph (bar chart or histogram) and justify the choice.
questions.modelAnswer: Favorite genre is categorical, so it gets a bar chart with separated bars — the categories have no numeric order or scale between them. Daily listening minutes is continuous quantitative data, so it gets a histogram with touching bars, because the classes sit on a continuous number line.

rubric.description: Pairs genre → bar chart and minutes → histogram
Justifies via categorical vs continuous data (separated vs touching bars)
